food_subgroup: Dense fudge-like sweet made by cooking chickpea flour (besan/gram flour) with ghee and sugar syrup; traditional South Indian mithai

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Mysore Pak. Closest parent concept is Indian besan-based sweet / gram flour confection (FOODON:03315595 — confection). No exact URI available; null used honestly.
ontology.foodex2_description: Confectionery — sugar confectionery (closest FoodEx2 parent for gram flour ghee fudge; no specific Mysore Pak code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Mysore Pak or Indian besan sweets. A039S (sugar confectionery) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Mysore Pak or equivalent Indian gram flour sweet identified.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of Mysore Pak weighs approximately 40 g and provides around 192 kcal, 11.2 g fat (6.8 g saturated), and 21.6 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mysore Pak / Besan-based Indian sweets
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides compositional data for besan-based Indian sweets including gram flour confections. Values for Mysore Pak are adapted from the ICMR-NIN besan sweet category and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chickpea flour (ID 175188) and ghee (ID 173410) to validate macro proportions. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data where ICMR-NIN does not report them. ICMR-NIN is an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate for gram flour ghee sweet; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Mysore Pak exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~45 (low-medium) based on: (1) chickpea flour / besan GI ~28–35 (University of Sydney GI Database, chickpea-based foods); (2) the very high ghee content (~28g fat/100g) substantially slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption, raising the effective GI of the sugar component toward medium-low; (3) analogous Indian gram flour sweets (besan ladoo, chickpea-based halwa) cluster in the GI 40–55 range in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). GL per 40g serving = (45 × 21.6g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.7. This is classified as low GL (≤10). Confidence: 0.9 (estimated, not directly measured).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high saturated fat from ghee (17 g/100 g); low sodium (50 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Mysore Pak is low in sodium (20 mg per 40 g piece) and poses no direct sodium-related hypertension risk, but the very high saturated fat content (6.8 g per piece) is a secondary cardiovascular concern for hypertensive patients with concurrent dyslipidaemia; limit to one piece on festive occasions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (40 g/100 g) offset by low-medium GI (~45) due to very high ghee fat content; glycemic load per piece ~9.7 (low)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite very high sugar content (~40 g/100 g), the extraordinary ghee content slows glucose absorption, yielding a low-medium GI (~45) and a low glycemic load per 40 g piece (~9.7, comparable to a medium apple); however, the high caloric density (192 kcal per piece) and saturated fat load make portion control essential — one piece on a festive occasion is clinically manageable, but multiple pieces are not.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (17 g/100 g = 6.8 g per piece); high caloric density (480 kcal/100 g); cholesterol 60 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mysore Pak is high in saturated fat (6.8 g per 40 g piece, approximately 34% of the recommended daily limit) and calorie-dense (192 kcal per piece); patients with established cardiovascular disease or hyperlipidaemia should treat this as an occasional festive food and limit intake to one small piece, as regular consumption would meaningfully increase saturated fat intake and cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat pro-inflammatory load; gram flour provides protein, folate, and iron relevant to RA management
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The very high saturated fat content from ghee (17 g/100 g) contributes to pro-inflammatory saturated fatty acid load relevant in rheumatoid arthritis; the gram flour component provides meaningful protein (7 g/100 g), folate (35 mcg/100 g), and iron (2 mg/100 g) which support RA nutritional management, but the ghee-sugar combination at cooking temperatures promotes advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mysore Pak (மைசூர் பாக் / मैसूर पाक) is one of South India's most iconic traditional sweets and a Deepavali staple in Singapore's Tamil and South Indian community. Originating in the royal kitchens of the Mysore Palace in Karnataka — legend attributes its invention to Kakasura Madappa, royal chef to the Mysore Maharaja — it is made from three primary ingredients: gram flour (besan / chickpea flour), ghee (clarified butter), and sugar. The preparation involves roasting gram flour in a heavy-bottomed pan, incorporating it into a hot sugar syrup, and gradually adding large quantities of melted ghee while stirring continuously. Two distinct styles exist: the traditional 'porous' Mysore Pak, which is firm with a honeycomb-like crumbly structure formed by rapid sugar crystallisation around the ghee-soaked besan matrix; and the 'soft Bangalore style', which is denser, smoother, fudge-like, and melts instantly in the mouth. Singapore's Indian community typically prefers the soft Bangalore style. At 480 kcal/100 g and 28 g fat per 100 g (predominantly saturated ghee fat at 17 g/100 g), Mysore Pak is extremely calorie-dense. The key clinical insight that surprises patients: despite very high sugar content (~40 g/100 g), the glycemic index is only approximately 45 (low-medium) because the extraordinary ghee content dramatically slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption. This yields a glycemic load of only ~9.7 per 40 g piece — comparable to a medium apple — making Mysore Pak glycemically more forgiving than its sugar content suggests. The caloric density (192 kcal per piece) and saturated fat load (6.8 g per piece) are the more clinically important counselling points. Mysore Pak is gluten-free and vegetarian. It is available at Mustafa Centre, Little India sweet shops, and is commonly home-made for Deepavali, Dasara, and South Indian weddings.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (approved primary regional source for Indian dishes) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data for chickpea flour and ghee. Minor micronutrients (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, water) are not reported by ICMR-NIN for this preparation and are set to -1. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) based on University of Sydney GI Database chickpea-based food data and peer-reviewed GI tables. All mandatory multilingual names are present. Confidence is rated high for macros and medium-high overall.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Mysore Pak not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish; individual ghee and besan fatty acid profiles exist but reliable composite values for the cooked preparation are not published. water_g set to -1: moisture content for Mysore Pak not reported in ICMR-NIN for this preparation. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: gram flour, ghee, and sugar contain negligible vitamin C; confirmed by USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: ghee contains negligible industrial trans fats; naturally occurring ruminant trans fats (CLA) are present at trace levels below reporting threshold per USDA FoodData Central ghee entry (ID 173410). 4. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate (not directly measured); confidence 0.9. No direct GI measurement for Mysore Pak exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 5. Portions: input contained duplicate 30g serving entries; consolidated to one canonical portion (1 piece = 40g, verified) and one secondary portion (1 serving = 30g). 6. FoodEx2 code A039S used as closest defensible parent (sugar confectionery); no specific Mysore Pak code exists. 7. Vitamin B12 (0.2 mcg/100g) is present due to ghee (dairy-derived); plausible per USDA ghee data. 8. Vitamin A (50 mcg RAE/100g) is sourced from ghee content; consistent with USDA FoodData Central ghee entry.
